relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: YHWH speaks Jubilee from Sinai.
The land itself is commanded to rest before YHWH.
Six years of sowing and pruning are permitted.
The seventh year becomes Sabbath of deep rest for the land.
What grows by itself is not harvested as private accumulation.
The land's Sabbath produce feeds household, worker, and sojourner.
Beast and wild animal share the resting land's food.
Seven Sabbaths of years are counted toward release.
The Jubilee trumpet sounds on the Day of Coverings.
Liberty is proclaimed to all inhabitants; property and clan ties are restored.
The fiftieth year refuses sowing, reaping, and accumulation.
Jubilee produce is holy and eaten from the field.
Each one returns to ancestral holding.
Buying and selling must not become mutual exploitation.
Land price is measured by harvest years remaining.
Price rises or falls only with the number of crops.
Do not wrong one another; fear your Divine.
Justice and statute make secure dwelling possible.
The land gives fruit, satisfaction, and security.
The people ask how they will eat while the land rests.
YHWH promises sixth-year abundance beyond one season.
Old produce sustains the people until new harvest comes.
The land cannot be sold forever because it belongs to YHWH.
Every holding remains open to redemption.
When a kinsperson becomes poor, the nearest redeemer restores the land.
A person who later gains means may redeem the holding.
The price is recalculated, and the seller returns home.
If redemption cannot come sooner, Jubilee returns the land.
A house in a walled city has a one-year redemption window.
After the year, the city house remains with the buyer.
Village houses remain joined to land and Jubilee return.
Levite city houses remain always redeemable.
Levite city property returns in Jubilee.
Levite pastureland cannot be permanently sold.
When your kinsperson's hand falters, strengthen them so they may live with you.
Do not profit from their desperation through interest or increase.
Money and food are not tools for crushing the poor.
The Liberator from Egypt grounds this economic command.
An impoverished Israelite is not to be worked as a slave.
He remains a hired worker or resident until Jubilee.
At Jubilee he and his children return to clan and land.
Those YHWH freed from Egypt are YHWH's servants, not human property.
No crushing rule is permitted over kin.
The ancient text allows foreign slaves, a grave moral limitation requiring rejection in modern use.
Resident-settler families are also exposed to ownership in the ancient system.
Inherited foreign slavery stands in tension with Exodus liberation and must not be normalized.
An Israelite sold to a wealthy sojourner still remains redeemable.
Kin may redeem him after the sale.
Close family or the person himself may provide redemption.
Redemption price is calculated by years of hired labor until Jubilee.
Many remaining years increase the redemption amount.
Few remaining years reduce the redemption amount.
The worker must not be ruled with crushing severity.
Even without earlier redemption, Jubilee releases him and his children.
Israel belongs in service to the Liberator, never as permanent human property.

contemplative_note: Leviticus 25 imagines an economy that periodically refuses permanence: land rests, debt loses its grip, families return, predatory profit is forbidden, and crushing rule is denied. Its foreign-slavery exception remains a serious moral fracture that must never be defended today.
